Senate Republicans, let’s be honest—who among you actually watched last week’s 39-hour judicial nomination spectacle? I’ll admit, I only caught glimpses of it. This exhausting display was orchestrated by Republicans, frustrated that Democrats refused to rubber-stamp four of Bush’s controversial judicial picks. Instead of constructive dialogue, they staged a prolonged political tantrum.  

Never mind that Democrats have already confirmed 168 of Bush’s nominees—apparently, that wasn’t sufficient for Republicans, who seem to believe they have an overwhelming mandate for such theatrics. The entire ordeal was nothing more than a wasteful exercise in partisan grandstanding.

When I look in the mirror today, I see a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State senator. I see all of that and a man who spent a quarter of his life in state prison—six years, to be precise—beginning as a teenager on Rikers Island for an act that nearly cost someone their life. However, what brought me from that past to my present wasn't the punishment I faced as a teenager in an adult prison or the severity of our legal system. Rather, it was the educational environment of a classroom that opened my eyes to possibilities I never imagined for myself or the justice system at large. Just a few weeks before my release on parole, a counselor encouraged me to enroll in a new college course being offered within the prison.

Advances in high-resolution optical imaging have been significantly driven by the widespread adoption of nonlinear fluorescence emission techniques. This study focuses on the anomalous saturation behavior of nitrogen vacancy defects in diamond, a phenomenon that has garnered considerable attention in recent years. Notably, our experiments revealed a pronounced reduction in fluorescence emission when subjected to high-power laser excitation, which in turn amplified the nonlinearity of the fluorescence signal and altered the spatial frequency distribution of the resulting fluorescence image. To extract high spatial frequency information, we employed a differential excitation protocol that involved modulating the power of the excitation laser. This approach yielded a substantial improvement in spatial resolution, with an approximate 1.6-fold enhancement compared to conventional confocal microscopy. Given the relative simplicity of the experimental setup and data processing requirements, we anticipate that this method will have far-reaching implications for enhancing the spatial resolution of sensing and biological labeling applications that utilize defects in solid-state materials.

Podcasts mark rise of diy radio an apple ipod or other digital music players can hold anything up to 10,000 songs, which is a lot of space to fill. But more and more ipod owners are filling that space with audio content created by an unpredictable assortment of producers. It is called "podcasting" and its strongest proponent is former mtv host and vj (video jockey) adam curry. Podcasting takes its name from the apple ipod, although you do not need an ipod to create one or to listen to a podcast. A podcast is basically an internet-based radio show which podcasters create, usually in the comfort of their own home. They need only a microphone, a pc, and some editing software. They then upload their shows to the internet and others can download and listen to them, all for free.

While inquiring about the most challenging aspects of psychology, one common response is that people often struggle with understanding emotions, citing their complexity and intricacies. "Emotions are extremely difficult," they might say, "I have no idea how they function." In contrast, thinking is often perceived as a more straightforward process, often reduced to a simplistic notion of logical reasoning. However, the real challenge lies in addressing the numerous problems that arise, such as, for instance, what we do about health. The other day, I came across an article that highlighted a staggering statistic  the largest single cause of disease in the Western world is likely hand hygiene.

Summer television used to be a wasteland filled with reruns, but now two exciting new shows have captured my attention. I've been watching both shows religiously over the past week. One of them, "Blow Out," features an unexpectedly flamboyant concept with hardly any visible LGBTQIA+ representation. It follows hairstylist Jonathan Antin, who is aiming to open a second branch of his widely successful West Hollywood salon, this time in Beverly Hills. Ordinarily, I wouldn't be interested at all. However, while flipping through channels, I happened upon Jonathan, who, with a mix of vanity and monotony, gazed at an empty space on Rodeo Drive and declared, "I am going to build here the greatest salon in the history of the world ever!" Strangely, I was hooked. Not since "Rocco" have I found a character this compelling on television, despite their apparent foolishness.

Deep learning has made significant strides in solving problems that were previously out of reach of heuristic algorithms. It has also been successfully applied to wireless communication systems, even in areas where well-established radio systems are already optimized and optimal algorithms exist for many tasks. While some advancements have been achieved by learning individual components of a receiver, a more effective approach is to jointly learn the entire receiver. However, this often results in a challenging non-linear problem, for which the optimal solution is difficult to implement. To address this challenge, we propose a deep fully convolutional neural network, deeprx, which processes the entire receiver pipeline from frequency domain signal stream to uncoded bits in a 5G-compliant manner. By constructing the input of the convolutional neural network in a very specific manner using both the data and pilot symbols, we facilitate accurate channel estimation and ensure the efficient processing of the receiver.

Allan Scott has made a promising start to his international career, finishing fourth in the 60m hurdles at the prestigious Jose M Cagigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid. The 22-year-old Scottish athlete, who hails from East Kilbride, is now setting his sights on a medal at the upcoming European Indoor Championships. Despite a slight dip in performance in the final, where he clocked 7.68 seconds after winning his heat in 7.64 seconds, Scott remains optimistic about his chances. "The experience was invaluable, and I'm definitely not ruling out a medal challenge next week," he said. The event was won by Spain's Felipe Vivancos, who equalled the national record, with Sweden's Robert Kronberg and Haiti's Dudley Dorival securing second and third place respectively.

In the primordial era, before the dawn of humanity, a celestial realm existed, comprising vast expanses of fields, plains, and gardens. Four brothers roamed this divine domain, their kinship limited to one another, as they were unaware of their parentage. One of these brothers, Deminan, stood apart from his siblings, his skin bearing the marks of painful scabs, and he pondered the reason for his singular affliction. One day, while the supreme being, Yaya, was absent from his gardens, Deminan and his brothers stealthily entered Yaya's abode. After feasting and exploring, they espied a colossal gourd suspended in the corner. However, as they endeavored to gaze within the gourd, they inadvertently dropped it.

Halloween writer debra hill dies screenwriter and producer debra hill, best known for her work on the 70s horror classic halloween, has died in los angeles aged 54. Hill, who had been suffering from cancer, co-wrote the 1978 film, which starred jamie lee curtis as a babysitter terrorised by a psychopath. Directed by john carpenter, it made over $60m (£31.3m) worldwide - a record for independent film at that time. Hill also worked with carpenter on escape from new york and the fog. Born in new jersey, hill began her career as a production assistant and worked her way through the ranks, becoming an assistant director and second-unit director before she began collaborating with carpenter. She was regarded by many as a pioneering woman in film, taking on jobs in the 70s that were more commonly taken by men.

Traditional understanding suggests that an electron primarily exhibits a single form of kinetic energy. However, could it possess additional variations related to its spin, charge, or interactions with other electrons? In one-dimensional (1D) systems, the Tomonaga-Luttinger-Liquid (TLL) model accurately describes the behavior of interacting electrons at low energies, though experimental observations beyond this linear range have been limited. In this study, we present our findings on many-body modes in 1D gated wires through tunneling spectroscopy techniques. We detected two distinct Fermi seas at elevated energies, corresponding to spin and charge excitations, along with the development of three additional 1D 'replica' modes that become more pronounced with shorter wire lengths. The effective interaction strength within the wires was modified by adjusting the degree of 1D inter-subband screening, exceeding a 45% change.

For nearly a quarter century, I've proudly served as a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. My team and I dedicate ourselves to protecting lives and property—responding to vehicle accidents, structural fires, and critical medical emergencies. As a woman leading in this traditionally male-dominated field, I've continually sought ways to enhance our community's safety. A decade ago, recognizing that modern threats extend beyond immediate crises, I pursued a nursing degree. This decision came from understanding that our greatest challenges are no longer single-incident disasters where firefighters can heroically intervene and depart. Today's complex emergencies require sustained medical expertise to address ongoing public health needs that persist long after the initial emergency response.

Brizzel to Defend 200m Title at AAA Championships in Sheffield

Paul Brizzel, the talented Irish sprinter, will be among eight of Ireland's European Indoor hopefuls competing in this weekend's AAA Championships in Sheffield. With his impressive performance in the British Trials, Brizzel is eager to defend his 200m title. The in-form athlete has been dominating the European circuit in recent weeks, and his recent ranking as the fastest 800m runner in the world this winter has solidified his position as a top contender in the Madrid championships.

Meanwhile, US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll, the only Irish athletes selected so far for the Europeans, will not compete in Sheffield. Cragg, ranked number one in the world 3000m ranking, will be focusing on his event in Madrid, while Carroll, ranked number three in the world 3000m ranking, has confirmed his participation in the European championships.

In-form James McIlroy will hope to confirm his place in the British team for Madrid by winning the 800m title in Sheffield.

You’ve been accused of a crime you did not commit. It’s impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you’re innocent anyway, you’ll likely be found guilty and executed. But if you confess, apologize, and implicate others for good measure, you’ll go free. Do you give a false confession— or risk a public hanging?this was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of salem, massachusetts between february 1692 and may 1693. They were the victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor— and a justice system that valued repentance over truth.salem was settled in 1626 by puritans, a group of english protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of salem. Battles with their native american neighbors and groups of french settlers were commonplace.

Online reviews are crucial for customers when it comes to making informed purchase decisions. Unfortunately, these reviews can be compromised by fake reviews or "spams" inserted by professional spammers. These spammers continue to develop more sophisticated and powerful tactics to evade existing detection methods. Capturing these spamming strategies is challenging as they evolve rapidly, vary between spammers and products, and are often unknown. Moreover, most current detection techniques prioritize accuracy but do not necessarily uphold the reliability of product evaluations. To solve these issues, we propose a minimax game framework where spammers and spam detectors tactically compete, focusing on pragmatic objectives beyond mere detection accuracy. The Nash equilibrium of this game yields stable detection systems that are robust against any combination of detection methods.

A novel, conserved current is introduced within the framework of an effective theory encompassing pions and vector mesons, grounded in the concept of hidden local symmetry. This current's associated charge is demonstrated to be equivalent to the skyrmion charge for all smooth configurations, while singular configurations can be identified as baryons with n_f=1, exhibiting charge under the newly proposed symmetry. Notably, vector mesons assume the role of Chern-Simons vector fields residing on the quantum Hall droplet that constitutes the n_f=1 baryon. We propose that this current provides the accurate effective representation of the baryon current at low energies, thereby offering a unified paradigm for the two distinct types of baryons. This unified framework enables a seamless transformation between the two baryon types, while Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a manifestation of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons, fostering a deeper understanding of the intricate relationships between these fundamental particles.

In a historic move, Israeli football club Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin is set to field four African players, including Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro, in the UEFA Cup. As the first Arab team to compete in European competition, Bnei Sakhnin will face off against English Premiership side Newcastle United in the first round. Murambadoro, who gained international recognition for his impressive performance at the African Nations Cup finals in Tunisia, played a crucial role in Bnei Sakhnin's 6-1 victory over Albania's Partizani Tirana in the previous round. Following a brief stint with South African club Hellenic, Murambadoro recently made the move to Israel, joining a team that has established itself as a champion of peace and coexistence. Based in Sakhnin, near Haifa, Bnei Sakhnin has built a reputation for promoting ethnic harmony and social unity, both on and off the pitch, having won the Israeli Cup final last season.

Where does all the carbon we release go? carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon.but over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock and some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock. And human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon.today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

The orientational dynamics within the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer—incorporating both mesogenic and functional side groups—were investigated through Kerr effect measurements and dielectric spectroscopy. This study revealed a novel finding: unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the electric birefringence relaxation in a mesogenic polymer melt above the nematic-isotropic transition temperature can be characterized by a superposition of multiple exponential decay processes. Among these, two dominant relaxation mechanisms were identified, which exhibit a temperature-dependent exchange of dominance across an approximately 50-degree range.  

Complementary dielectric spectroscopy further resolved two distinct orientational relaxation processes: the first arising from the rotational motion of the mesogenic side groups and the second linked to the segmental dynamics of the polymer backbone. This dual-method approach provides unprecedented insight into the hierarchical relaxation behavior of comb-shaped nematic polymers in their isotropic state.

The estimation of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset is a crucial step in most dimensionality reduction techniques. This article introduces intrinsic, an R package that offers state-of-the-art likelihood-based estimators for the intrinsic dimension of a dataset. The package includes novel methods, specifically the two-nn, gride, and hidalgo models, which are designed to be easily accessible through a set of high-level, user-friendly functions that leverage efficient, low-level routines. Intrinsic dimension estimation encompasses two categories  homogeneous and heterogeneous models. The package provides functions for both homogeneous and heterogeneous methods, including the two-nn and gride models, which can be applied under both frequentist and Bayesian frameworks. Additionally, it features hidalgo, a Bayesian mixture model, with an efficient Gibbs sampler implementation.

A comprehensive review of the human gait was conducted by generalizing existing models in the literature, drawing from the works of Keller and Kockshenev. This hybrid model combines a metabolic energy minimization strategy with a non-linear description of the movement of the mass center's libration, aiming to replicate the behavior of the walk-run transition. The experimental data obtained for various speed regimes reveal that the perimeter of the trajectory of the mass center is a crucial factor in quantifying this dynamic process. In collaboration with the research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratories at the Universidad de Manuela Beltrán in Bogotá, Colombia, an experimental procedure was developed and implemented.

Here is the polished text:
Two twin domes, two radically opposed design cultures. One is made of thousands of steel parts, the other of a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One is imposed on the environment, the other creates it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by her. Michelangelo said that when he looked at raw marble, he saw a figure struggling to be free. The chisel was Michelangelo's only tool. But living things are not chiseled. They grow. And in our smallest units of life, our cells, we carry all the information that's required for every other cell to function and to replicate. Tools also have consequences. At least since the industrial revolution, the world of design has been dominated by the rigors of manufacturing and mass production.

A Georgia woman has filed a lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that the company's printer ink cartridges are designed with a secret expiration date, resulting in unnecessary replacements. According to the complaint, a microchip embedded in the cartridge deceives the printer into believing it requires refilling, even when a substantial amount of ink remains. The lawsuit aims to represent a class of individuals who have purchased HP inkjet printers in the United States since February 2001. HP, the world's leading printer manufacturer, has declined to comment on the matter. While HP's ink cartridges utilize chip technology to detect low ink levels and prompt users to replace them, the lawsuit claims that this technology also triggers an automatic shutdown of the cartridges on a predetermined date, regardless of the actual ink levels.

The 22-year-old Scot finished fourth in the 60m hurdles at the Jose M Cagigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid. "It was definitely a learning curve, and I certainly haven't ruled out challenging for a medal next week," said the East Kilbride athlete. Felipe Vivancos of Spain won the race, equaling the Spanish record. Sweden's Robert Kronberg was second, with Haiti's Dudley Dorival in third. A cross-party coalition of parliamentarians has intensified the push for reform of the House of Lords by releasing a comprehensive proposal for its transformation. The draft legislation, unveiled by the group, envisions a streamlined second chamber, with 70% of its members elected by the people. This move comes as a response to the long-standing stalemate on reform, which has persisted since 1999, when the removal of 600 hereditary peers from the House of Lords was implemented. Despite the impasse, the group remains optimistic about garnering support for the abolition of the remaining 92 hereditary peers. The government had previously shelved plans to eliminate these hereditary peers, citing the unlikelihood of success due to opposition from within the House of Lords. Prime Minister Tony Blair has consistently emphasized the need for consensus on reform, suggesting that any changes to the House of Lords, including potential modifications to its powers, may be outlined in the Labour Party's forthcoming manifesto.

**Polished Version:**  

Though fate denied us the bonds of marriage or children, though we were never bound by vows or titles, a part of you will forever dwell within me. My love for you was boundless, and its echo lingers still—a quiet, persistent whisper in my heart.  

I will always cherish those fleeting nights when we were one, our bodies and souls intertwined beneath the vast, starlit sky by the shore, or the memory of your lips meeting mine in the rain. Even now, when sorrow pierces me like shards of dark glass, you are there—that fragment of you, alive within me, bleeding the love that once burned so fiercely… and never truly faded.  

For her.

Life can get overwhelmingly hectic at times, but there's always room for leisure activities that promise to alleviate stress. I've recently discovered that my skills in 8-ball and ping pong are, shall we say, less than impressive. My colleague Rick, affectionately known as "the Canuck," consistently outmaneuvers me on the ping pong table conveniently located in our building's front lobby, where we often squeeze in a quick game. Last night, I participated in an 8-ball tournament at our recreation tent, aptly dubbed "the hot spot," which boasts an impressive array of amenities, including a popcorn machine, multiple TVs, a jukebox, DVD rentals, a pool table, a dartboard, and, of course, a ping pong table. Although my tournament run was short-lived, I managed to hold my own until I made the critical mistake of scratching on the eight ball. Observations of thermally driven transverse vibration of a photonic crystal waveguide (pcw) are reported. The pcw consists of two parallel nanobeams with a 240 nm vacuum gap between the beams. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion to phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode (gm) of the pcw for probe frequencies far from and near to the dielectric band edge. Since our pcw has been designed for near-field atom trapping, this research provides a foundation for evaluating possible deleterious effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of pcws. Longer term goals are to achieve strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the gms of the pcw, thereby enabling opto-mechanics at the quantum level with atoms, photons, and phonons.

The newly appointed Health Minister for Wales, Dr. Brian Gibbons, has emphasized the need to learn from England’s strategies in addressing healthcare waiting lists. On his inaugural day in office following the dismissal of Jane Hutt, Dr. Gibbons acknowledged significant challenges but affirmed that the foundational elements for progress remain intact.  

However, opposition parties, including Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats, have called for a shift in policy under his leadership. In her defense, Ms. Hutt highlighted her tenure’s broader achievements, asserting that waiting times constituted merely 10% of the overall health agenda.  

Before assuming his ministerial role, Dr. Gibbons served as a general practitioner in Blaengwynfi, located in the Upper Afan Valley, and later as the Assembly Member for Aberavon. He noted that NHS staff are seeking stability after years of extensive transformation and restructuring.  

Among the most pressing issues during Ms. Hutt’s five-and-a-half-year tenure as Wales’ inaugural Health Minister was the persistent challenge of lengthy waiting lists—a key concern that continues to shape the healthcare debate.

Republican Party Faces Criticism Over Voter Registration at Naturalization Ceremonies

Naturalization ceremonies in Jacksonville will be relocated due to concerns following allegations that Republican activists were registering new citizens as members of their party. Reported by Gary Fineout and Alfonso Chardy, the citizenship ceremony initially proceeded as expected, with nearly 200 attendees taking part and becoming new Americans. Just before leaving the event on June 29, an immigration official encouraged these new citizens to visit a voter registration table—an ordinary feature at such ceremonies. However, this table was different: Republican volunteers were distributing the forms, and the party affiliation box had already been pre-checked to register each new voter as a Republican. This appeared suspicious to Linda Cross, who was present to witness her husband, Dario Cruz, take his citizenship oath.

Entanglement is a crucial resource for processing quantum information, playing a pivotal role in quantum computing and quantum communication. A widely employed tool for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, which is characterized by a guaranteed positive measurement outcome for separable states and a negative outcome for certain entangled states. However, as the Hilbert space dimension increases exponentially with system size, it is extremely challenging to construct an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/2$ white noise. In contrast, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states necessitates only two local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/3$ white noise, making it a more practical and scalable solution for entanglement detection.

Israeli club Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin looks to Africa for four African players, including Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro, who are all set to compete in the Uefa Cup. Bnei Sakhnin, the first Arab side to play in a European competition, will face off against English Premier League side Newcastle United in the first round. Murambadoro, who made a name for himself at the African Nations Cup finals in Tunisia, helped Bnei Sakhnin defeat Albania's Partizani Tirana 6-1 in the previous round. Based in Sakhnin, which is near Haifa, Bnei Sakhnin is known for its strong ethos and commitment to promoting peace and cooperation within Israel.

The El Paso pipeline has announced an open season to test market interest in a mainline expansion and to address regulatory concerns, which is expected to relieve the apparent capacity constraint at the California border. The pipeline's announcement comes as El Paso has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to enormous demand and skyrocketing prices in California. The pipeline has attracted bids for 14.4 billion cubic feet per day, with the last open season for 1.2 billion cubic feet per day, indicating a strong interest in the project. In a January 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" now facing the California gas market.

The past week has been an arduous ordeal, with the relocation to my new classroom consuming a significant amount of time and energy. From Tuesday to Thursday, I dedicated myself to completing the move, taking into consideration the room's advantages, including its larger size and proximity to my grade level team. These factors will undoubtedly enhance my teaching experience, but they also necessitated a substantial investment of time in preparing the space for the upcoming year. In hindsight, had I been aware of the impending move prior to the end of the previous school year, I would have taken the opportunity to declutter and discard outdated books and supplies, thereby streamlining the transition process. However, as it was, I found myself burdened with the task of transporting and unpacking all of my belongings, followed by an extensive purging process. I am deeply grateful to my colleague, Jill, who generously lent her support during this challenging period. Notably, she was still utilizing my former classroom for her summer teaching schedule, and she, along with her students, kindly assisted in relocating the majority of my belongings, greatly alleviating my workload.

When asked about which aspect of psychology is challenging, and prompted with "What about thinking and emotions?" most people typically respond that emotions are incredibly difficult to comprehend—complex beyond their understanding. In contrast, they tend to view thinking as more straightforward, equating it to some form of logical reasoning. However, this perception overlooks the true complexity of the situation. For example, consider this scenario: How do we address health issues? Recently, I read an intriguing claim stating that handshaking is possibly the greatest single contributor to disease spread in Western countries.

Ordinary differential equation (ode) is a mathematical model used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering with its intuitive appeal to modeling. Despite ode's wide usage in modeling, frequent absence of their analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ode parameters from the data, especially when the model has lots of variables and parameters. This paper proposes a bayesian ode parameter estimating algorithm which is fast and accurate even for models with many parameters. The proposed method approximates an ode model with a state-space model based on equations of a numeric solver. It allows fast estimation by avoiding computations of a whole numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained by a variational bayes method, more specifically, the approximate riemannian conjugate gradient method (honkela et al.

Fall 1974: i was attempting for what felt like the 13th time to fight my way through the second chapter of my high school algebra book. I was not then nor am i now a math person. I hate math. I am math phobic. You get the picture. So as i struggled to make sense out of algebra the doorbell rang. I put down my book and went to open the door. No one was there. Great. I returned to my studies and just as i was finding a little glimmer of understanding buried in my sub conscious the doorbell rang again. Bam, just like that my breakthrough disappeared. I went to the door and once again no one was there. Just what a struggling math student needs, an uninvited practical joker.

Last night was particularly frustrating. I ended up discarding the sweater I was working on, even though I had completed about 14 inches. I lost approximately $10 worth of yarn, with each skein costing $8. Unfortunately, by the time I reached the armholes, there were too many stitches, and the mohair kept tangling as I knitted. Trying to undo the rows, known as frogging, was ineffective because the yarn frequently snapped. Despite my usual tendency to swear in such situations, I remained surprisingly calm. Instead, I just listened to the second season of "Sex and the City" (thanks to my library) while I unraveled my work in a rather gloomy manner. Eventually, I grew tired and decided to throw it away. Looking back, I guess I can say I learned a lot from attempting to make this sweater.

The spreadsheet below provides an implicit commodity breakdown based on the effective date of four different rates for the same rate schedule (schedule DR). The rate schedule, effective from August 2000 through September 2000, yields a significantly larger commodity charge, as SDG&E customers will receive a lump-sum reimbursement from SDG&E over those two months. SDG&E customers will expect lower electric bills. Assuming that the lump-sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average rate for June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents per kilowatt-hour, and the implicit commodity average from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cents per kilowatt-hour. The average implicit commodity rate from June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cents per kilowatt-hour for deliveries starting September 1, 2000, the average would still be 6.9 cents per kilowatt-hour.

Direpack is a comprehensive Python package that unifies cutting-edge statistical dimension reduction techniques under a single, cohesive framework. By categorizing methods into projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M-estimators, the package provides a structured approach to data analysis. Furthermore, it offers a range of regularized regression estimators that leverage these reduced dimension spaces, spanning from traditional principal component regression to sparse partial robust M regression. To facilitate seamless data preparation and visualization, direpack includes an array of classical and robust pre-processing tools, such as generalized spatial signs, as well as specialized plotting and cross-validation utilities. Notably, the package is designed to be fully compatible with the scikit-learn API, allowing its estimators to be effortlessly integrated into statistical and machine learning pipelines, thereby enhancing the overall workflow.

The direpack package aims to establish a set of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques into the python universe as a single, consistent package. The dimension reduction methods included resort into three categories: projection pursuit based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust m estimators for dimension reduction. As a corollary, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are provided as well, ranging from classical principal component regression up to sparse partial robust m regression. The package also contains a set of classical and robust pre-processing utilities, including generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Finally, direpack has been written consistent with the scikit-learn api, such that the estimators can flawlessly be included into (statistical and/or machine) learning pipelines in that framework.

In 1903, the president of the united states took a three-day camping trip in california’s yosemite valley. President theodore roosevelt slept in a grove of towering sequoia trees, camped in a snowstorm, and spent hours talking around the campfire with his host and guide, conservationist john muir. Roosevelt famously loved the outdoors, but muir had invited him there for more than just camping: yosemite was in danger.though yosemite became protected land in 1864, the valley was still at risk for overdevelopment in 1903. It was at the heart of a decades-old struggle to set aside land for both preservation and public use— two goals that were much easier said than done.the battle over yosemite began with the 1849 gold rush, when miners surged west, seeking gold in the sierra nevada foothills.

The orientational dynamics in the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematogenic polymer with mesogenic and functional side groups were studied using the Kerr effect and dielectric spectroscopy. For the first time, it was found that in a mesogenic polymer, in contrast to low-molecular-weight mesogens, the relaxation of the electric birefringence of a melt above the temperature of the nematic-isotropic phase transition can be presented by a sum of several exponential processes, two of which play a decisive role. These main processes replace each other in a temperature range of approximately fifty degrees. Dielectric spectroscopy also made it possible to distinguish two processes of orientational relaxation  the first is due to the rotation of the side mesogenic groups, and the second is associated with the motion of the main chain segments, which is a crucial aspect of the polymer's behavior in this regime.

El Paso Natural Gas Pipeline to Conduct Open Season for Mainline Expansion
El Paso Natural Gas, a subsidiary of Kinder Morgan, Inc., will conduct an open season for its Mainline Expansion project to test market interest in additional firm transportation capacity. The pipeline has announced that it will offer up to 14.4 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) of firm transportation capacity for a 10-year term, subject to certain conditions.
The open season is scheduled to run from February 1 to March 10, and interested shippers are invited to submit bids for the available capacity. The pipeline has advised that it will evaluate bids based on a variety of factors, including the shipper's transportation requirements, the distance of the gas to the pipeline, and the shipper's creditworthiness.
The Mainline Expansion project is designed to relieve the apparent capacity constraint at the California border, where El Paso Natural Gas has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to high demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a January 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project

Everyone has their own guilty pleasure when it comes to one movie or series of movies. Mine is the 'Evil Dead' series. If it wasn't already apparent, I have one of the largest collections of'stuff from that movie series' that I've accumulated over the years.

As for musical talents, I can play the bass guitar. In fact, I'm quite skilled at rocking out to it.

In terms of vices, I'd say that my biggest money-wasting habits are my compulsive buying of DVDs or any new technology. I have to admit, I struggle with this tendency.

I don't have any phobias, allergies, or compulsive behaviors, but I do have one unusual habit  I insist on keeping all my money in my wallet in order from least to greatest at all times. This habit is quite peculiar, but it's something I've developed over the years.

Finally, the age-old question  how much wood could a woodchuck chuck if a woodchuck could chuck wood? The answer, of course, is because I said so.

Yukos Returns to U.S. Courts: The Russian oil and gas company, Yukos, is scheduled to appear in a U.S. court on Thursday as it continues its struggle for survival. The Russian authorities are dismantling the firm to settle a $27 billion (£14 billion) tax debt. In response, Yukos sought bankruptcy protection in the U.S., hoping to leverage international business law to halt the compulsory sale of its crucial oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas. Despite these efforts, the unit was sold for $9.4 billion to the state-run oil company Rosneft, but not before the state auction was interrupted. Yukos' legal team now argues that the auction breached U.S. bankruptcy laws. The company and its principal shareholders have pledged to pursue any entity that acquires its assets, utilizing every available legal avenue.

Emerging paradigms, innovative approaches, and key challenges in the characterization and manipulation of open quantum systems through sequential methodologies are presented. This work demonstrates how complementary strategies—combining iterative quantum measurements with dynamical decoupling control sequences—enable three critical advancements: (1) the stabilization and preservation of quantum coherence through quantum Zeno dynamics, effectively mitigating environmental decoherence; (2) the derivation of closed-form expressions for state-transfer probabilities in stochastic measurement sequences; and (3) the precision reconstruction of environmental noise spectra via frequency-domain orthogonalization of quantum sensor filter functions. These breakthroughs collectively expand our capacity to probe and engineer open quantum systems, paving the way for transformative developments across quantum technologies.

In this paper, we study the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to nonconvexity, there exist multiple local minima that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We show that all local minima share the same form of structure that partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture, in the sense that each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to the setting where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition, and are valid even when the number of components is over-or under-specified. For gaussian mixtures with three components, we obtain sharper results in terms of the scaling with the separation between the components.

Mefin Davies, the prospective future hooker for Wales, is probably going to continue with Gloucester, the English team, despite interest from the Neath-Swansea Ospreys. BBC Wales reports that although the Ospreys are keen on the 32-year-old player, Davies would rather remain at Gloucester. Davies, who shone in Wales' RBS Six Nations victory over England on Saturday, currently has a one-year contract with Kingsholm. However, he has shown his value to the Zurich Premiership team, making it likely that he will receive a contract extension next season. Following the dissolution of the Celtic Warriors, Davies found himself without a team and had to sign a semi-professional contract with Neath RFC. While he did get playing time with the Ospreys per the Wales management's request, he expressed his frustration with his treatment before joining Gloucester.

The fields of optomechanics and electromechanics have achieved remarkable milestones, enabling the preparation of macroscopic mechanical oscillators in fundamental quantum states—including quantum ground states, quadrature squeezed states, and entangled motional states. Beyond pushing the boundaries of quantum behavior in increasingly larger and more tangible objects, these advancements have sparked innovative applications, such as harnessing mechanical oscillators for quantum information processing, communication, and ultra-precise force sensing beyond the standard quantum limit.  

However, the predominantly linear interaction between mechanical motion and light or electric fields restricts access to richer quantum states—such as cat states or energy squeezed states—limiting the full potential of these systems. Early theoretical work in optomechanics proposed overcoming this constraint by establishing strong quadratic coupling between motion and light. While experimental progress has demonstrated quadratic coupling in optomechanical setups, the realization of nonclassical motional states remains an outstanding challenge in the field.

Imagine if you could plug your brain into a machine that would bring you ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If you were given the choice to sign up for that kind of existence, would you? that's the question philosopher robert nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the experience machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world in which scientists have developed a machine that would simulate real life while guaranteeing experiences of only pleasure and never pain. The catch? you have to permanently leave reality behind, but you'll hardly know the difference. Your experiences will be indistinguishable from reality. Life's natural ups and downs will just be replaced with an endless series of ups. Sounds great, right?

Over the past 48 hours, I've been beset by debilitating episodes of exhaustion, leaving me feeling utterly drained. The fatigue is so overwhelming that it's become a struggle to stay awake. Last evening, I succumbed to it, retreating to the comfort of my home and surrendering to a deep slumber. It wasn't until my mother's voice pierced the darkness, calling me around 8:30, that I stirred back to life. Disoriented and groggy, I lay there for a few moments, trying to regain my bearings in the darkness. The only sound was my mother's voice, a lifeline that helped me reconnect with reality. I'm still trying to pinpoint the cause of this exhaustion. In stark contrast, the morning brought a welcome respite, with a gentle drizzle that seemed to wash away some of my fatigue. I've since learned that the rain has been relentless in Calcutta, with the city experiencing a three-day downpour.

The pursuit of causal inference from observational data is a primary objective in many health and social science disciplines. However, the academic community has traditionally been skeptical about data analysis with a causal objective. The introduction of the term 'data science' offers a historic opportunity to redefine data analysis in a way that naturally incorporates causal inference from observational data. Like others before, we categorize the scientific contributions of data science into three distinct classes of tasks  description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction (which encompasses causal inference). A clear classification of data science tasks is essential to discuss the data, assumptions, and analytics required to successfully accomplish each task. We argue that a failure to adequately describe the role of subject-matter expert knowledge in data analysis is a root cause of widespread misconceptions about data science.

This study explores the realm of multi-agent discrete-event systems, where agents operate under partial observation and can be categorized into distinct groups based on their isomorphic state transition structures. By leveraging this template-based approach, a novel, scalable supervisory framework is developed, which decouples the state size and computational complexity from the number of agents involved. Notably, this framework remains invariant to changes in the agent population, eliminating the need for recalibration when agents are added or removed. Building upon previous research, this work extends the existing results to accommodate partial observation, introducing necessary conditions for ensuring safety and maximal permissiveness in the scalable, least restrictive supervisor. A comprehensive example is provided to demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed supervisory synthesis methodology.

Nestled within the pristine expanse of Canada's boreal forest—the world's largest and most ecologically intact woodland—lies an environmental and industrial project of unprecedented scale and destruction. Spanning the northern reaches of the country, including Labrador, this region shelters the planet's last great migratory caribou population: the George River herd, with nearly 400,000 individuals. Though I failed to spot a single caribou during my visit, their presence is unmistakably marked by the scattered antlers dotting the landscape.  

The boreal forest is a sanctuary of unparalleled biodiversity, particularly its vast network of wetlands—among Earth's most threatened yet vital ecosystems. These natural wonders serve as the planet's lungs and kidneys, purifying air and water while capturing immense quantities of greenhouse gases. Beyond their ecological functions, they sustain an extraordinary array of wildlife, making them indispensable to the health of our planet.

"The Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) was invited by the U.S. State Department to observe the upcoming presidential election. The monitoring team, drawn from the OSCE’s Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights, marks the first time such international observers will be present for a U.S. election.  

Meanwhile, a group of thirteen congressional Democrats—including four representatives from New York—has formally requested that U.N. Secretary-General Kofi Annan deploy international monitors to Florida to oversee the voting process. However, I respectfully disagree with this proposal. Available information suggests that the OSCE’s role is to study the U.S. electoral system rather than to supervise it—a distinction that renders the earlier call for U.N. intervention both politically motivated and unnecessary.

Broadband fuels online expression fast web access is encouraging more people to express themselves online, research suggests. A quarter of broadband users in britain regularly upload content and have personal sites, according to a report by uk think-tank demos. It said that having an always-on, fast connection is changing the way people use the internet. More than five million households in the uk have broadband and that number is growing fast. The demos report looked at the impact of broadband on people's net habits. It found that more than half of those with broadband logged on to the web before breakfast. One in five even admitted to getting up in the middle of the night to browse the web. More significantly, argues the report, broadband is encouraging people to take a more active role online.

Menstruation: A Natural Process Weighed Down by Stigma

Menstruation, a biological process that is essential for life, has been shrouded in secrecy and shame for centuries. Despite the fact that over 800 million women worldwide experience it every month, it remains a taboo topic in many cultures. As I speak to you now, women around the world are silently enduring the physical and emotional challenges of menstruation, hiding their sanitary products in brown paper bags and rushing to the bathroom to deal with the pain and discomfort in private.

The stigma surrounding menstruation has a profound impact on women's lives. It can make it difficult to discuss openly and honestly about their periods, leading to feelings of isolation and embarrassment. The shame and secrecy surrounding menstruation can also lead to a lack of understanding and support for women's reproductive health, perpetuating harmful attitudes and practices.

Before the world of humans began, there was the world of the gods, made of fields, plains and gardens. Four brothers wandered this celestial realm. They had no family other than each other— they didn’t even know who their parents were. One of the brothers, deminan, looked different from the others. His skin was covered in painful scabs, and he wondered why he alone had been marked with this affliction.one day, while the supreme spirit yaya was out in his gardens, deminan and his brothers snuck into yaya’s house. After feasting and exploring, they spotted a giant gourd hanging in the corner. But as they tried to look inside the gourd, they dropped it.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2d) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2d fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2d fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity v$_{\rm c}$. We measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

Home phones face unclear future the fixed line phone in your home could soon be an endangered species. Research by handset maker nokia shows that more and more people are using their mobile phone for every call they make or take. According to the study, more than 45 million people in the uk, germany, us and south korea now only use a mobile. It showed that people keep their fixed line phone because call charges are lower, but most of those questioned said the future was definitely mobile. The nokia-sponsored research showed that mobiles and fixed phones were used for different purposes. Home phones were used for longer calls but conversations on mobiles tended to be shorter, between mobiles and to friends. In the uk 69% of those questioned said they turned to their fixed phone because it was still cheaper to use than a mobile.
